rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Relief From Claim Requirement [Gov. Code Sec. 946.6]
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 18, 2015, Line 1, PETITIONER ANA FERNANDEZ' Motion For Relief From Claim Requirement [Gov. Code Sec. 946.6]. DENIED. Plaintiff's explanation that failure to timely file a claim against CCSF was because of a calendaring error is inadequate and does not rise to excusable neglect. There is no showing of reasonable diligence. Plaintiff's counsel states that he was retained on July 1, 2014. Given that he was retained and not merely considering if he would take the case or not, he fails to explain why calendaring was even necessary as opposed to promptly filing. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EG)

rulings.Case Title: DANIEL GULAK VS. WHITE PACIFIC SECURITIES INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On First Amended Petition To Confirm Contractual Arbitration Award
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 18, 2015, Line 2, PETITIONER DANIEL GULAK'S Hearing On First Amended Petition To Confirm Contractual Arbitration Award. Plaintiff's petition to confirm contractual arbitration award is GRANTED on the condition that it makes clear the entire arbitration award: (1) White Pacific is liable for $200,000.00; (2) Robert Nelson is liable for $50,000.00; (3) Items one and two are not awarded jointly and severally; (4) Patrick Nelson is not liable; (5) Robert Nelson's request for expungement of his CRD record is denied; and (6) any and all relief not specifically addressed is denied. This court GRANTS to plaintiff $800 in attorneys' fees and $862 in costs. rulings.Case Title: CHRISTINA M SAGONOWSKY VS. CHRISTINE TOUR-SARKISSIAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Protective Order And Request For Monetary Sanction Against Plaintiff Christina Sagonowsky
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 18, 2015, Line 1, DEFENDANTS CHRISTINE TOUR-SARKISSIAN, PHIL FOSTER, ABIGAIL MORRIS, PAUL TOUR-SARKISSIAN, AND TOUR-SARKISSIAN LAW OFFICES' Motion For Protective Order And Request For Monetary Sanction Against Plaintiff Christina Sagonowsky. Pro Tem Judge Anthony Schoenberg, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Motion for Protective by defendants Christine Tour-Sarkissian, Paul Tour-Sarkissian, Tour-Sarkissian Law Offices, LLP, Abigail Morris and Phil Foster ("Defendants") is granted. Defendants shall not be obligated to provide any further responses to Plaintiffs' written discovery requests that are the subject of the motion. Plaintiffs served 44 sets of written discovery on Defendants containing 1,640 document requests, 1,128 special interrogatories and 849 RFAs, as well as 11 sets of form interrogatories. "When discovery requests are grossly overbroad on their face, and hence do not appear reasonably related to a legitimate discovery need, a reasonable inference can be drawn of an intent to harass and improperly burden." Obregon v. Superior Court, 67 Cal. App. 4th 424, 431 (1998). The profoundly voluminous and duplicative discovery that Plaintiffs served on Defendants raises such an inference. Accordingly, Defendants' motion for protective order is well taken. While Defendants' meet and confer efforts were less than prodigious, "the level of effort that is reasonable is different in different circumstances, and may vary with the prospects for success." Id. at 432-433. Here, Defendants' meet and confer efforts were adequate under the circumstances. Furthermore, Defendants' request for sanctions in the amount of $373.90 is granted, as Plaintiffs did not act with substantial justification in connection with the subject discovery. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to tschoenberg@fbm.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: EDDIE SUMMERFIELD VS. GUIZHEN LUO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion By Defendants David Graff And Guizhen Luo To Quash Service Of Summons For Lack Of Jurisdiction
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 18, 2015, Line 3, DEFENDANT DAVID GRAFF, GUIZHEN LUO'S Motion To Quash Service Of Summons For Lack Of Jurisdiction. Defendants' Motion to Quash is DENIED. The Court rejects Defendant's argument that there are insufficient contacts between Defendants and California to support the constitutional exercise of jurisdiction. Defendants admit that Defendant Graff visited California in March 2008 to speak with Plaintiff about the fiancée visa process; Plaintiff's Declaration alleges that he paid Graff $10,000 at this meeting and was the victim of fraud as a result, and that Defendant Luo was a participant in the fraud as well. A state has a special interest in exercising judicial jurisdiction over those who commit torts within its territory. (Keeton v. Hustler Magazine, Inc. (1984) 465 U.S. 770, quoting Leeper v. Leeper (1974) 114 N.H. 294, 298.) Defendants shall file a responsive pleading to the Complaint within 15 days. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EG)

rulings.Case Title: EDDIE SUMMERFIELD VS. GUIZHEN LUO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion By Defendants David Graff And Guizhen Luo To Set Aside And To Vacate Entry Of Default
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 18, 2015, Line 3, DEFENDANT DAVID GRAFF, GUIZHEN LUO'S Motion To Set Aside And To Vacate Entry Of Default. Defendants' Motion to Vacate Default is GRANTED. Declarations establish Defendants were more likely than not unaware of the lawsuit against them until May 4, 2015, and were never served, personally or otherwise. Vacating the judgment is therefore appropriate under Code of Civil Procedure §§ 473(d) and/or 473.5. The Court rejects Defendant's argument that there are insufficient contacts between Defendants and California to support the constitutional exercise of jurisdiction. Defendants admit that Defendant Graff visited California in March 2008 to speak with Plaintiff about the fiancée visa process; Plaintiff's Declaration alleges that he paid Graff $10,000 at this meeting and was the victim of fraud as a result, and that Defendant Luo was a participant in the fraud as well. A state has a special interest in exercising judicial jurisdiction over those who commit torts within its territory. (Keeton v. Hustler Magazine, Inc. (1984) 465 U.S. 770, quoting Leeper v. Leeper (1974) 114 N.H. 294, 298.) Defendants shall file a responsive pleading to the Complaint within 15 days. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EG)

rulings.Case Title: CHARLES C. CHOW et al VS. TED WIN WONG et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 18, 2015, Line 4, DEFENDANT CHINESE CONSOLIDATED BENEVOLENT ASSOCIATION'S MOTION For Order Appointing Referee. Pro Tem Judge Anthony Schoenberg, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Nominal Defendant Chinese Consolidated Benevolent Association's ("CCBA") Motion for Order Appointing Referee is denied. The Taggares v. Superior Court, 62 Cal. App. 4th 94, 105 (1998) opinion discusses the high standard for appointing a discovery referee where the parties do not agree to it: "Unless both parties have agreed to a reference, the court should not make blanket orders directing all discovery motions to a discovery referee except in the unusual case where a majority of factors favoring reference are present. These include: (1) there are multiple issues to be resolved; (2) there are multiple motions to be heard simultaneously; (3) the present motion is only one in a continuum of many; (4) the number of documents to be reviewed (especially in issues based on assertions or privilege) make the inquiry inordinately time-consuming." CCBA has not made an adequate showing to justify a discovery referee under this standard. The discovery issues discussed in CCBA's motion concern one deposition -- the CCBA custodian of records. The only motion referenced is a motion to compel that deposition. It appears from Plaintiffs' opposition that there have also been some disagreements over scheduling of depositions. Based on this record, the Judge Pro Tem cannot conclude that a "majority of factors favoring reference" are present. Rather, these appear to be issues that the parties can work out on their own or, barring that, address through regular law and motion practice. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to tschoenberg@fbm.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: TRIIO, LLC VS. MANUEL J. CHAVEZ ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 18, 2015, Line 10, CROSS DEFENDANT TRIIO, LLC'S DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT. Plaintiff's Demurrer overruled in part and sustained in part. Cross Defendant's demurrer to the 1st cause of action is Overruled. 16 of the cross-complaint states "Topolski changed the wage structure to wages of $4,543.28 per month to Chavez and the balance as a commission for his services payable in the amount of $6,394.22 to Vhantom." Thus, P has adequately pled a modification. Further, 2 of the complaint alleges Chavez to be the sole member and 100% owner of Vhantom. Cross Defendant's demurrer to the 3rd cause of action is Overrulled. The complaint alleges Cross Defendant Topolski exercised total dominion and control over Triio and held such a unity of identity and interest that the acts of Triio complained of should be deemed to be his and the entity disregarded. (See Cross Complaint 4.) Therefore D's allegations are sufficient to plead alter ego theory. Cross Defendant's demurrer to the 4th cause of action is Overrulled. The Cross-Complaint alleges Triio has breached the promissory note. (See Cross Complaint 12.) Cross Defendant's demurrer to the 5th cause of action is Sustained with 20 days leave to amend. Unauthorized access to stored communications that have not been properly alleged in the 5th COA. Cross Defendant's demurrer to the 6th cause of action is Overrulled. The Cross Complaint alleges Defendant Chavez had a reasonable expectation of privacy in email communications, that was violated by Topolski, and as a result Defendant Chavez suffered harm. (See Cross Complaint 39 - 44.) Cross Defendant's demurrer to the 8th cause of action is Overruled. Corporations Code §17704.09(d) states: "A member shall discharge the duties to a limited liability company and the other members under this title or under the operating agreement and exercise any rights consistent with the obligat faith and fair dealing." Here the Cross-Complaint alleges Cross Defendant's breached their obligation of good faith and fair dealing as to Defendant Chavez. (See Cross Complaint 48 - 50.) Cross Defendant's demurrer to the 9th cause of action is Overruled. Civil Code §45 states "[l]ibel is a false and unprivileged publication by writing, printing, picture, effigy, or other fixed representation to the eye, which exposes any person to hatred, contempt, ridicule, or obloquy, or which causes him to be shunned or avoided, or which has a tendency to injure him in his occupation." The Cross Complaint alleges Topolski violated Civil Code §45 through a written statement declaring Defendant Chavez acted unethically in his business. Cross Defendant's demurrer to the 10th cause of action is Sustained with 20 days leave to amend. Cross Defendant makes reference to the provision for indemnification of members, however, this provision is not attached to the Cross Complaint, therefore the cross complaint is uncertain as to the 10th Cause of Action. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EG)
